questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the number of molecules present in $1 \text{ cm}^3$ of an ideal gas at $0^\circ \text{C}$ and a pressure of $10^{-5} \text{ mm}$ of mercury.
questions.solution:
Given,
Volume $V = 1 \text{ cm}^3 = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
Temperature $T = 0^\circ \text{C} = 273 \text{ K}$
Pressure $P = 10^{-5} \text{ mm of Hg} = \dfrac{10^{-5}}{760} \times 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 1.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/m}^2$

Using the ideal gas equation,
$PV = nRT = \dfrac{N}{N_A} RT$

where $N$ is the number of molecules and $N_A$ is Avogadro's number.

$N = \dfrac{PV N_A}{RT}$

Substituting the values,
$N = \dfrac{1.33 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-6} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{8.314 \times 273}$

$N = \dfrac{8.009 \times 10^{14}}{2269.7}$

$N \approx 3.53 \times 10^{11}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the volume of $1$ mole of an ideal gas at STP.
questions.solution:
At Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP), the temperature is $T = 273.15 \text{ K}$ and the pressure is $P = 1 \text{ atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$.

Using the ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$ for $1 \text{ mole}$ of gas:

$V = \dfrac{nRT}{P} = \dfrac{1 \times 8.314 \times 273.15}{1.013 \times 10^5} \approx 0.0224 \text{ m}^3$

Alternatively, it is a standard fact that $1 \text{ mole}$ of an ideal gas occupies $22.4 \text{ L}$ at STP.

Converting liters to cubic meters:

$V = 22.4 \text{ L} = 22.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 0.0224 \text{ m}^3$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A gas cylinder features walls capable of withstanding a maximum pressure of $1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$. Inside, it holds a gas at $8.0 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ and $300 \text{ K}$. The cylinder is heated steadily. Assuming no change in volume, determine the temperature at which the cylinder will break.
questions.solution:
Given initial pressure $P_1 = 8.0 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ and initial temperature $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$.

The maximum pressure the cylinder can withstand is $P_2 = 1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$.

Since the volume of the cylinder remains constant, Gay-Lussac's law is applicable:

$\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$

Substituting the given values:

$\dfrac{8.0 \times 10^5}{300} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^6}{T_2}$

$T_2 = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^6 \times 300}{8.0 \times 10^5}$

$T_2 = \dfrac{10}{8} \times 300$

$T_2 = 375 \text{ K}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the mass of $1 \text{ cm}^3$ of oxygen maintained at STP.
questions.solution:
Volume of $1$ mole of an ideal gas at STP is $22.4 \text{ L} = 22400 \text{ cm}^3$.

Molar mass of oxygen gas ($O_2$) is $32 \text{ g}$.

Mass of $22400 \text{ cm}^3$ of oxygen at STP is $32 \text{ g}$.

Mass of $1 \text{ cm}^3$ of oxygen at STP is $\dfrac{32}{22400} \text{ g}$.

$\dfrac{32}{22400} \text{ g} = \dfrac{1}{700} \text{ g} \approx 1.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g} = 1.43 \text{ mg}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a vessel of volume $0.02 \text{ m}^3$, $2 \text{ g}$ of hydrogen is sealed and maintained at $300 \text{ K}$. Determine the pressure inside the vessel.
questions.solution:
Volume $V = 0.02 \text{ m}^3$

Mass of hydrogen gas $m = 2 \text{ g}$

Molar mass of hydrogen gas $M = 2 \text{ g/mol}$

Number of moles $n = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1 \text{ mol}$

Temperature $T = 300 \text{ K}$

Using the ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$

$P = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$

Substituting the given values:

$P = \dfrac{1 \times 8.314 \times 300}{0.02}$

$P = 1.247 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$

The closest value among the options is $1.24 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the number of molecules of an ideal gas present in a volume of $1.000 \text{ cm}^3$ at STP.
questions.solution:
At STP, $1 \text{ mole}$ of an ideal gas occupies a volume of $22.4 \text{ L}$ or $22400 \text{ cm}^3$.

The number of moles in $1.000 \text{ cm}^3$ of the gas is:

$n = \dfrac{1}{22400} \text{ mol}$

The number of molecules is given by $N = n \times N_A$, where Avogadro's number $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$.

$N = \dfrac{1}{22400} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}$

$N = \dfrac{6.022}{2.24} \times 10^{19} \approx 2.69 \times 10^{19}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the molecular weight of an ideal gas if its density at STP is $1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g cm}^{-3}$.
questions.solution:
Given density of the ideal gas, $d = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g cm}^{-3} = 1.25 \text{ g L}^{-1}$.

At standard temperature and pressure (STP), the molar volume of an ideal gas is $V_m = 22.4 \text{ L mol}^{-1}$.

The molecular weight $M$ of the gas can be calculated using the relation:

$M = d \times V_m$

Substituting the given values:

$M = 1.25 \text{ g L}^{-1} \times 22.4 \text{ L mol}^{-1} = 28.0 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a cylindrical tube with adiabatic walls is equipped with a diathermic separator. An external mechanism allows the separator to be slid inside the tube. An ideal gas is introduced into both compartments at equal pressures and equal temperatures, with the separator initially in equilibrium at the midpoint. The separator is subsequently slid to a location where it partitions the tube in the ratio of $1:3$. Determine the ratio of the pressures in the two sections of the vessel.
questions.solution:
Let the initial volume of each compartment be $V_0$. The total volume of the cylindrical tube is $2V_0$.

Initially, both compartments contain an ideal gas at the same pressure $P_0$ and temperature $T_0$. According to the ideal gas law, the number of moles in each compartment is equal:
$n_1 = n_2 = n = \dfrac{P_0 V_0}{R T_0}$

The separator is then slid to partition the tube in a $1:3$ ratio. The new volumes of the two compartments are:
$V_1 = \dfrac{1}{1+3} \times 2V_0 = \dfrac{V_0}{2}$
$V_2 = \dfrac{3}{1+3} \times 2V_0 = \dfrac{3V_0}{2}$

Since the separator is diathermic, it allows heat exchange between the two compartments, ensuring they reach thermal equilibrium. Thus, the final temperatures of both compartments will be equal, say $T_f$.

Applying the ideal gas equation to both compartments in the final state:
$P_1 V_1 = n R T_f$
$P_2 V_2 = n R T_f$

Equating the two expressions for $n R T_f$, we get:
$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$

Rearranging to find the ratio of the pressures:
$\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$

Substituting the values of $V_1$ and $V_2$:
$\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{3V_0}{2}}{\dfrac{V_0}{2}} = \dfrac{3}{1}$

Therefore, the ratio of the pressures in the two sections is $3:1$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature and pressure at Darjeeling are $15.0^\circ \text{C}$ and $72.0 \text{ cm}$ of mercury, while at Siliguri they are $35.0^\circ \text{C}$ and $76.0 \text{ cm}$ of mercury. Determine the ratio of the air density at Siliguri to the air density at Darjeeling.
questions.solution:
Using the ideal gas equation, $P = \dfrac{\rho R T}{M}$

$\Rightarrow \rho = \dfrac{P M}{R T}$

$\Rightarrow \rho \propto \dfrac{P}{T}$

For Darjeeling:

$P_1 = 72.0 \text{ cm of Hg}$

$T_1 = 15.0^{\circ}\text{C} = 288 \text{ K}$

For Siliguri:

$P_2 = 76.0 \text{ cm of Hg}$

$T_2 = 35.0^{\circ}\text{C} = 308 \text{ K}$

Ratio of density at Siliguri to Darjeeling is:

$\dfrac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \times \dfrac{T_1}{T_2}$

$\dfrac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \dfrac{76.0}{72.0} \times \dfrac{288}{308}$

$\dfrac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \dfrac{19}{18} \times \dfrac{72}{77} = \dfrac{76}{77} \approx 0.987$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the rms speed of hydrogen molecules within a sample of hydrogen gas at $300 \text{ K}$. Also, find the temperature at which the rms speed is twice this calculated speed.
questions.solution:
The root mean square speed of gas molecules is given by:

$v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3RT}{M}}$

For hydrogen gas ($\text{H}_2$), $M = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg mol}^{-1}$. Substituting $T = 300 \text{ K}$ and $R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$:

$v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \times 8.314 \times 300}{2 \times 10^{-3}}}$

$v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{3.7413 \times 10^6} \approx 1934 \text{ m s}^{-1} \approx 1930 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

Let $T'$ be the temperature at which the rms speed is twice the initial speed. Thus:

$v_{\text{rms}}' = 2 v_{\text{rms}}$

$\sqrt{\dfrac{3RT'}{M}} = 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{3RT}{M}}$

Squaring both sides:

$\dfrac{3RT'}{M} = 4 \left( \dfrac{3RT}{M} \right)$

$T' = 4T$

$T' = 4 \times 300 = 1200 \text{ K}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the average translational kinetic energy of air molecules is given as $0.040 \text{ eV}$ ($1 \text{ eV} = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$), determine the temperature of the air. Take the Boltzmann constant to be $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J K}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The average translational kinetic energy of a gas molecule is given by $E = \dfrac{3}{2}kT$.

Given $E = 0.040 \text{ eV} = 0.040 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 6.4 \times 10^{-21} \text{ J}$.

Substituting the values into the formula:

$6.4 \times 10^{-21} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times T$

$T = \dfrac{2 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-21}}{3 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23}}$

$T = \dfrac{12.8 \times 100}{4.14} \approx 309.18 \text{ K}$

The closest value among the options is $310 \text{ K}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For an oxygen sample at $300 \text{ K}$, determine the average time required for a molecule to cover a distance equal to the diameter of the earth.
questions.solution:
The average speed of a gas molecule is given by:

$v_{avg} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$

For an oxygen ($O_2$) molecule at $300 \text{ K}$:

$R = 8.314 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$
$T = 300 \text{ K}$
$M = 32 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg mol}^{-1}$

Substituting the values:

$v_{avg} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \times 8.314 \times 300}{\pi \times 32 \times 10^{-3}}} \approx 445.5 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

The diameter of the earth is $D = 2 \times 6400 \text{ km} = 12800 \text{ km} = 12.8 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$.

The average time required to cover this distance is:

$t = \dfrac{D}{v_{avg}} = \dfrac{12.8 \times 10^6}{445.5} \approx 28731 \text{ s}$

Converting the time into hours:

$t = \dfrac{28731}{3600} \text{ hours} \approx 7.98 \text{ hours} \approx 8 \text{ hours}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the average magnitude of linear momentum for a helium molecule in a sample of helium gas at $0^\circ \text{C}$. The mass of a helium molecule is $6.64 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$ and the Boltzmann constant is $1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J K}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The average magnitude of linear momentum of a gas molecule is given by:

$\langle p \rangle = m \langle v \rangle$

where $\langle v \rangle$ is the average speed of the molecule. The formula for average speed from the kinetic theory of gases is:

$\langle v \rangle = \sqrt{\dfrac{8kT}{\pi m}}$

Thus, the average magnitude of linear momentum is:

$\langle p \rangle = m \sqrt{\dfrac{8kT}{\pi m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8mkT}{\pi}}$

Given values:
Mass of helium molecule, $m = 6.64 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$
Boltzmann constant, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J K}^{-1}$
Temperature, $T = 0^\circ \text{C} = 273 \text{ K}$

Substituting these values into the expression:

$\langle p \rangle = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \times 6.64 \times 10^{-27} \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 273}{\pi}}$

$\langle p \rangle = \sqrt{\dfrac{20012.4 \times 10^{-50}}{3.1416}}$

$\langle p \rangle = \sqrt{6370.15 \times 10^{-50}}$

$\langle p \rangle = 79.81 \times 10^{-25} \text{ kg m s}^{-1}$

$\langle p \rangle \approx 8.0 \times 10^{-24} \text{ kg m s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose the molecules of a hydrogen sample possess a mean speed equal to the mean speed of the molecules of a helium sample. Determine the ratio of the temperature of the hydrogen sample to the temperature of the helium sample.
questions.solution:
The mean speed of gas molecules is given by $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$.

Given that the mean speed of hydrogen molecules is equal to the mean speed of helium molecules:

$\sqrt{\dfrac{8RT_{H_2}}{\pi M_{H_2}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT_{He}}{\pi M_{He}}}$

Squaring both sides, we get:

$\dfrac{T_{H_2}}{M_{H_2}} = \dfrac{T_{He}}{M_{He}}$

$\dfrac{T_{H_2}}{T_{He}} = \dfrac{M_{H_2}}{M_{He}}$

The molar mass of hydrogen gas ($H_2$) is $2$ g/mol and the molar mass of helium gas ($He$) is $4$ g/mol.

$\dfrac{T_{H_2}}{T_{He}} = \dfrac{2}{4} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

The ratio of the temperature of the hydrogen sample to the temperature of the helium sample is $1 : 2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A fixed diathermic separator partitions a vessel as shown in the figure. Different ideal gases are contained in the two parts. The rms speed of the molecules in the left part is equal to the mean speed of the molecules in the right part. Evaluate the ratio of the mass of a molecule in the left part to the mass of a molecule in the right part.
questions.solution:
Since the vessel is partitioned by a diathermic separator, heat can flow between the two parts until thermal equilibrium is reached. Therefore, the temperatures of the gases in both parts are equal. Let $T_L = T_R = T$.

The root mean square (rms) speed of gas molecules is given by $v_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3kT}{m}}$, where $k$ is the Boltzmann constant, $T$ is the temperature, and $m$ is the mass of a single molecule.

The mean (average) speed of gas molecules is given by $v_{mean} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8kT}{\pi m}}$.

According to the problem, the rms speed of the molecules in the left part is equal to the mean speed of the molecules in the right part:
$(v_{rms})_L = (v_{mean})_R$

Substituting the expressions for the speeds:
$\sqrt{\dfrac{3kT}{m_L}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8kT}{\pi m_R}}$

Squaring both sides:
$\dfrac{3kT}{m_L} = \dfrac{8kT}{\pi m_R}$

Canceling $kT$ from both sides, we get:
$\dfrac{3}{m_L} = \dfrac{8}{\pi m_R}$

Rearranging to find the ratio of the mass of a molecule in the left part to that in the right part:
$\dfrac{m_L}{m_R} = \dfrac{3\pi}{8}$

Using the value of $\pi \approx 3.1416$:
$\dfrac{m_L}{m_R} = \dfrac{3 \times 3.1416}{8} = \dfrac{9.4248}{8} \approx 1.178$

Rounding to two decimal places, the ratio is $1.18$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the ratio of the mean speed of hydrogen molecules to the mean speed of nitrogen molecules within a sample containing a mixture of the two gases.
questions.solution:
The mean speed of gas molecules is given by $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$, where $R$ is the universal gas constant, $T$ is the absolute temperature, and $M$ is the molar mass of the gas.

Since the hydrogen and nitrogen molecules are in the same mixture, they are at the same temperature $T$.

The ratio of the mean speed of hydrogen molecules to that of nitrogen molecules is:

$\dfrac{v_{H_2}}{v_{N_2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{M_{N_2}}{M_{H_2}}}$

The molar mass of hydrogen ($H_2$) is $2 \text{ g/mol}$ and the molar mass of nitrogen ($N_2$) is $28 \text{ g/mol}$.

Substituting these values:

$\dfrac{v_{H_2}}{v_{N_2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{28}{2}} = \sqrt{14} \approx 3.74$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the approximate number of collisions per second experienced by a molecule within a hydrogen sample at STP. The mean free path (the average distance a molecule travels between successive collisions) is given as $1.38 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}$.
questions.solution:
The number of collisions per second (collision frequency) experienced by a molecule is given by the ratio of the average speed of the molecules to their mean free path.

$Z = \dfrac{v_{avg}}{\lambda}$

For hydrogen gas at STP, the temperature is $T = 273 \text{ K}$ and the molar mass is $M = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}$. The average speed $v_{avg}$ is given by:

$v_{avg} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$

Substituting the standard values ($R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol K)}$):

$v_{avg} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \times 8.314 \times 273}{\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}}$

$v_{avg} \approx 1700 \text{ m/s} = 1.7 \times 10^5 \text{ cm/s}$

Given the mean free path $\lambda = 1.38 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}$, the number of collisions per second is:

$Z = \dfrac{1.7 \times 10^5}{1.38 \times 10^{-5}}$

$Z \approx 1.23 \times 10^{10} \text{ s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Hydrogen gas is confined within a closed vessel at $1 \text{ atm}$ ($100 \text{ kPa}$) and $300 \text{ K}$. Determine the mean speed of the molecules.
questions.solution:
The mean speed of gas molecules is given by the formula:

$v_{\text{mean}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$

For hydrogen gas ($H_2$), the molar mass is $M = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg mol}^{-1}$.

Substituting $T = 300 \text{ K}$ and $R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$:

$v_{\text{mean}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \times 8.314 \times 300}{\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}}$

$v_{\text{mean}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{19953.6}{6.283 \times 10^{-3}}}$

$v_{\text{mean}} = \sqrt{3.17 \times 10^6} \approx 1780 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Hydrogen gas is confined within a closed vessel at $1 \text{ atm}$ ($100 \text{ kPa}$) and $300 \text{ K}$. Assume that the molecules strike the wall with their mean speed, making an average angle of $45^\circ$ with the wall. Calculate the number of molecules striking each square metre of the wall per second.
questions.solution:
Mean speed of hydrogen molecules is given by:

$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$

Substituting the given values:

$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \times 8.314 \times 300}{\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}} \approx 1782 \text{ m/s}$

Mass of one hydrogen molecule is:

$m = \dfrac{M}{N_A} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} \approx 3.32 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$

When a molecule strikes the wall at an angle of $45^\circ$ with the wall, the component of velocity perpendicular to the wall is $v \sin 45^\circ = \dfrac{v}{\sqrt{2}}$.

The change in momentum of each molecule upon elastic collision is:

$\Delta p = 2 m v_{\perp} = 2 m \left(\dfrac{v}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \sqrt{2} m v$

Let $n$ be the number of molecules striking per unit area per second. The total change in momentum per unit area per second is equal to the pressure $P$.

$P = n \Delta p = n \sqrt{2} m v$

Rearranging for $n$:

$n = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{2} m v}$

Substituting $P = 100 \text{ kPa} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$:

$n = \dfrac{10^5}{\sqrt{2} \times 3.32 \times 10^{-27} \times 1782} \approx 1.2 \times 10^{28}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the morning, at an air temperature of $20^\circ C$, air is pumped into an automobile tyre's tube to a pressure of $200 \text{ kPa}$. Over the course of the day, the temperature increases to $40^\circ C$ and the tube expands by $2\%$. Determine the pressure of the air in the tube at this new temperature.
questions.solution:
Using the ideal gas equation:

$\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$

The initial pressure is $P_1 = 200 \text{ kPa}$, initial temperature is $T_1 = 20^\circ\text{C} = 293 \text{ K}$, and initial volume is $V_1 = V$.

The final temperature is $T_2 = 40^\circ\text{C} = 313 \text{ K}$ and the final volume is $V_2 = V + 0.02V = 1.02V$.

Substituting the values into the equation:

$\dfrac{200 \times V}{293} = \dfrac{P_2 \times 1.02V}{313}$

$P_2 = \dfrac{200 \times 313}{293 \times 1.02}$

$P_2 = \dfrac{62600}{298.86} \approx 209.46 \text{ kPa}$

The pressure of the air in the tube is approximately $209 \text{ kPa}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During its manufacturing, an electric bulb of volume $250 \text{ cc}$ is sealed at a pressure of $10^{-3} \text{ mm}$ of mercury and a temperature of $27^\circ C$. Determine the number of air molecules contained in the bulb. (Take the Avogadro constant $= 6 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$, density of mercury $= 13600 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, and $g = 10 \text{ m s}^{-2}$)
questions.solution:
Given:
Volume of the bulb, $V = 250 \text{ cc} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
Pressure, $P = 10^{-3} \text{ mm of Hg} = 10^{-6} \text{ m of Hg}$
Density of mercury, $\rho = 13600 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$
Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 10 \text{ m s}^{-2}$
Temperature, $T = 27^\circ \text{C} = 300 \text{ K}$

The pressure in Pascals is:
$P = h \rho g = 10^{-6} \times 13600 \times 10 = 0.136 \text{ Pa}$

Using the ideal gas equation:
$PV = nRT = \dfrac{N}{N_A}RT$

Rearranging for the number of molecules $N$:
$N = \dfrac{PVN_A}{RT}$

Substituting the values (using $R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$):
$N = \dfrac{0.136 \times 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 6 \times 10^{23}}{8.314 \times 300}$

$N = \dfrac{0.136 \times 250 \times 2 \times 10^{17}}{8.314}$

$N = \dfrac{68 \times 10^{17}}{8.314} \approx 8.17 \times 10^{15}$

The closest value among the options is $8 \times 10^{15}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Equal masses of air are sealed inside two vessels having volumes $V_0$ and $2V_0$. When the first vessel is maintained at a temperature of $300 \text{ K}$ and the second at $600 \text{ K}$, what is the ratio of the pressures in the two vessels?
questions.solution:
Using the ideal gas equation, $PV = nRT$

Since equal masses of air are taken, the number of moles $n$ is the same for both vessels.

For the first vessel:
$P_1 V_0 = nR(300)$
$P_1 = \dfrac{300nR}{V_0}$

For the second vessel:
$P_2 (2V_0) = nR(600)$
$P_2 = \dfrac{600nR}{2V_0} = \dfrac{300nR}{V_0}$

The ratio of pressures is:
$\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{300nR}{V_0}}{\dfrac{300nR}{V_0}} = \dfrac{1}{1}$

Thus, the ratio is $1:1$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A closed metal jar of volume $1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$ is filled with oxygen at a pressure of $1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ and a temperature of $400 \text{ K}$. A small leak is present in the jar. The atmospheric pressure is $1.0 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ and the atmospheric temperature is $300 \text{ K}$. Determine the mass of the gas that escapes by the time the pressure and temperature inside the jar become equal to those of the surroundings.
questions.solution:
The initial number of moles of oxygen gas in the jar is given by the ideal gas equation:

$n_1 = \dfrac{P_1 V}{R T_1}$

The final number of moles of oxygen gas in the jar is:

$n_2 = \dfrac{P_2 V}{R T_2}$

The number of moles of gas that escaped is:

$\Delta n = n_1 - n_2 = \dfrac{V}{R} \left( \dfrac{P_1}{T_1} - \dfrac{P_2}{T_2} \right)$

Substituting the given values $V = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$, $P_1 = 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $T_1 = 400 \text{ K}$, $P_2 = 1.0 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $T_2 = 300 \text{ K}$, and $R \approx \dfrac{25}{3} \text{ J mol}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$:

$\Delta n = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{25/3} \left( \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^5}{400} - \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^5}{300} \right)$

$\Delta n = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{25} \left( 375 - \dfrac{1000}{3} \right)$

$\Delta n = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{25} \left( \dfrac{1125 - 1000}{3} \right)$

$\Delta n = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{25} \times \dfrac{125}{3} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}$

The molar mass of oxygen gas ($O_2$) is $M = 32 \text{ g/mol}$.

The mass of the escaped gas is:

$\Delta m = \Delta n \times M = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol} \times 32 \text{ g/mol} = 0.16 \text{ g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An air bubble of radius $2.0 \text{ mm}$ forms at the bottom of a $3.3 \text{ m}$ deep river. Determine the radius of the bubble as it reaches the surface. Assume atmospheric pressure $= 1.0 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ and density of water $= 1000 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$.
questions.solution:
Let the pressure at the surface be $P_2 = 1.0 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ and the pressure at the bottom be $P_1$.

Using the relation for hydrostatic pressure, $P_1 = P_2 + \rho g h$.

Substituting the given values and taking $g = 10 \text{ m s}^{-2}$:

$P_1 = 1.0 \times 10^5 + 1000 \times 10 \times 3.3 = 1.33 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$

Assuming the temperature remains constant, Boyle's law is applicable:

$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$

$P_1 \left( \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r_1^3 \right) = P_2 \left( \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r_2^3 \right)$

$r_2^3 = \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} r_1^3$

$r_2^3 = \dfrac{1.33 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^5} \times (2.0)^3$

$r_2^3 = 1.33 \times 8 = 10.64 \text{ mm}^3$

Since $(2.2)^3 = 10.648$, taking the cube root gives:

$r_2 \approx 2.2 \text{ mm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Air is pumped into the tubes of a cycle rickshaw at a pressure of $2 \text{ atm}$. At this pressure, the volume of each tube is $0.002 \text{ m}^3$. One of the tubes suffers a puncture, causing its volume to reduce to $0.0005 \text{ m}^3$. Determine the number of moles of air that have leaked out. Assume the air behaves as an ideal gas and the temperature remains constant at $300 \text{ K}$.
questions.solution:
Initial number of moles of air in the tube is given by the ideal gas equation $n_1 = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{R T}$.

Using $P_1 = 2 \text{ atm} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $V_1 = 0.002 \text{ m}^3$, $T = 300 \text{ K}$, and $R = \dfrac{25}{3} \text{ J mol}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$:

$n_1 = \dfrac{2 \times 10^5 \times 0.002}{\dfrac{25}{3} \times 300} = \dfrac{400}{2500} = 0.16 \text{ mol}$

After the puncture, the pressure inside the tube drops to atmospheric pressure, so $P_2 = 1 \text{ atm} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$. The final volume is given as $V_2 = 0.0005 \text{ m}^3$.

Final number of moles of air remaining in the tube:

$n_2 = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{R T} = \dfrac{10^5 \times 0.0005}{\dfrac{25}{3} \times 300} = \dfrac{50}{2500} = 0.02 \text{ mol}$

Number of moles of air that have leaked out:

$\Delta n = n_1 - n_2 = 0.16 - 0.02 = 0.14 \text{ mol}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A closed container initially holds $0.040 \text{ g}$ of He at $100.0^\circ \text{C}$. The container is subsequently heated. Ignoring the expansion of the container, determine the temperature at which the internal energy has increased by $12 \text{ J}$.
questions.solution:
The number of moles of He gas is given by $n = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{0.040}{4} = 0.01 \text{ mol}$.

Helium is a monatomic gas, so its molar heat capacity at constant volume is $C_V = \dfrac{3}{2} R$.

The change in internal energy is given by $\Delta U = n C_V \Delta T$.

Substituting the given values:

$12 = 0.01 \times \dfrac{3}{2} \times 8.314 \times \Delta T$

$\Delta T = \dfrac{12 \times 2}{0.01 \times 3 \times 8.314} = \dfrac{800}{8.314} \approx 96.2 \text{ K}$

Since a change in temperature in Kelvin is equal to the change in Celsius, $\Delta T = 96.2^\circ \text{C}$.

The final temperature is $T_f = T_i + \Delta T = 100.0^\circ \text{C} + 96.2^\circ \text{C} = 196.2^\circ \text{C} \approx 196^\circ \text{C}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A vessel holds $1.60 \text{ g}$ of oxygen and $2.80 \text{ g}$ of nitrogen. The temperature is kept at $300 \text{ K}$ and the volume of the vessel is $0.166 \text{ m}^3$. Determine the pressure of the mixture.
questions.solution:
Number of moles of oxygen, $n_{O_2} = \dfrac{1.60}{32} = 0.05 \text{ mol}$

Number of moles of nitrogen, $n_{N_2} = \dfrac{2.80}{28} = 0.10 \text{ mol}$

Total number of moles in the mixture, $n = n_{O_2} + n_{N_2} = 0.05 + 0.10 = 0.15 \text{ mol}$

Using the ideal gas equation, $PV = nRT$

$P = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$

Substituting the given values and taking $R = 8.3 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$:

$P = \dfrac{0.15 \times 8.3 \times 300}{0.166}$

$P = \dfrac{373.5}{0.166} = 2250 \text{ N/m}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Air is contained at a constant temperature within a vertical cylinder of height $100 \text{ cm}$. The top is sealed by a light, frictionless piston. Atmospheric pressure is equivalent to $75 \text{ cm}$ of mercury. Mercury is slowly poured onto the piston. Determine the maximum height of the mercury column that can be placed on the piston.
questions.solution:
Let $A$ be the cross-sectional area of the cylinder.

Initial pressure of the enclosed air, $P_1 = 75 \text{ cm}$ of Hg.
Initial volume of the air, $V_1 = 100 A$.

Let $x$ be the maximum height of the mercury column poured onto the piston. The final pressure of the air becomes $P_2 = (75 + x) \text{ cm}$ of Hg.

At the maximum height of the mercury column, the total height of the air column and the mercury column will be exactly equal to the height of the cylinder. Thus, the final length of the air column is $(100 - x) \text{ cm}$.

Final volume of the air, $V_2 = (100 - x) A$.

Since the temperature remains constant, Boyle's Law is applicable:

$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$

$75 \times 100 A = (75 + x)(100 - x) A$

$7500 = 7500 + 25x - x^2$

$x^2 - 25x = 0$

$x(x - 25) = 0$

Since $x > 0$ for the poured mercury, we get $x = 25 \text{ cm}$.

Therefore, the maximum height of the mercury column that can be placed on the piston is $25 \text{ cm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two vessels $A$ and $B$ with rigid walls containing ideal gases are shown in the figure. The pressure, temperature, and volume are $p_A, T_A,$ and $V$ in vessel $A$, and $p_B, T_B,$ and $V$ in vessel $B$. The vessels are subsequently connected through a small tube. When equilibrium is achieved, which of the following relations is satisfied by the final pressure $p$ and temperature $T$?
questions.solution:
From the ideal gas equation, the initial number of moles in vessel $A$ and vessel $B$ are:

$n_A = \dfrac{p_A V}{R T_A}$

$n_B = \dfrac{p_B V}{R T_B}$

The total number of moles before mixing is:

$n_{initial} = n_A + n_B = \dfrac{V}{R} \left( \dfrac{p_A}{T_A} + \dfrac{p_B}{T_B} \right)$

After the vessels are connected, the total volume becomes $V + V = 2V$. For the final equilibrium state with pressure $p$ and temperature $T$, the total number of moles is:

$n_{final} = \dfrac{p(2V)}{RT}$

Since the total number of moles is conserved, $n_{initial} = n_{final}$:

$\dfrac{p(2V)}{RT} = \dfrac{V}{R} \left( \dfrac{p_A}{T_A} + \dfrac{p_B}{T_B} \right)$

Dividing both sides by $\dfrac{2V}{R}$ gives:

$\dfrac{p}{T} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{p_A}{T_A} + \dfrac{p_B}{T_B} \right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An open container of volume $50 \text{ cc}$ holds air (mean molecular weight $= 28.8 \text{ g}$) and is exposed to the atmosphere where the pressure is $100 \text{ kPa}$. The container is placed in a bath containing melting ice ($0^\circ\text{C}$). Determine the mass of the air in the container when thermal equilibrium is established.
questions.solution:
Given parameters:
Volume of the container, $V = 50 \text{ cc} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
Pressure, $P = 100 \text{ kPa} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
Temperature, $T = 0^\circ\text{C} = 273.15 \text{ K}$
Mean molecular weight of air, $M = 28.8 \text{ g/mol}$
Universal gas constant, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol K)}$

Using the ideal gas equation:
$PV = nRT$

Since the number of moles $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$, we can write:
$PV = \dfrac{m}{M}RT$

Rearranging for mass $m$:
$m = \dfrac{PVM}{RT}$

Substituting the given values:
$m = \dfrac{10^5 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 28.8}{8.314 \times 273.15}$

$m = \dfrac{5 \times 28.8}{2270.97}$

$m = \dfrac{144}{2270.97} \approx 0.0634 \text{ g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An open container of volume $50 \text{ cc}$ holds air (mean molecular weight $= 28.8 \text{ g}$) and is exposed to the atmosphere where the pressure is $100 \text{ kPa}$. The container is placed in a bath containing boiling water ($100^\circ\text{C}$). Determine the mass of air in the container once thermal equilibrium is reached.
questions.solution:
Using the ideal gas equation, $PV = nRT$

Since $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$, we have $PV = \dfrac{m}{M}RT$

Rearranging for mass $m$:

$m = \dfrac{PVM}{RT}$

Given values:
$P = 100 \text{ kPa} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
$V = 50 \text{ cc} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
$M = 28.8 \text{ g/mol}$
$T = 100^\circ\text{C} = 373 \text{ K}$
$R = 8.314 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$

Substituting the values:

$m = \dfrac{10^5 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 28.8}{8.314 \times 373}$

$m = \dfrac{5 \times 28.8}{3101.122}$

$m = \dfrac{144}{3101.122} \approx 0.0464 \text{ g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas sample of $0.177 \text{ g}$ occupies $1000 \text{ cm}^3$ at STP. Determine the rms speed of the gas molecules.
questions.solution:
Density of the gas, $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{0.177 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}}{10^{-3} \text{ m}^3} = 0.177 \text{ kg/m}^3$

At standard temperature and pressure (STP), the pressure is $P \approx 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$.

The root mean square speed of the gas molecules is given by:

$v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3P}{\rho}}$

Substituting the values:

$v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \times 10^5}{0.177}} \approx \sqrt{1.69 \times 10^6} = 1300 \text{ m/s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform tube, closed at one end, contains a $10 \text{ cm}$ long pellet of mercury. When the tube is held vertically with its closed end pointing upward, the trapped air column has a length of $20 \text{ cm}$. Determine the length of the trapped air column when the tube is inverted such that the closed end faces downward. The atmospheric pressure is given as $75 \text{ cm}$ of mercury.
questions.solution:
Let the atmospheric pressure be $P_0 = 75 \text{ cm}$ of Hg and the length of the mercury pellet be $h = 10 \text{ cm}$.

When the tube is held vertically with the closed end pointing upward, the open end is downward. The pressure of the trapped air is:

$P_1 = P_0 - h = 75 - 10 = 65 \text{ cm}$ of Hg

The initial length of the air column is $L_1 = 20 \text{ cm}$.

When the tube is inverted so that the closed end faces downward, the open end is upward. The pressure of the trapped air becomes:

$P_2 = P_0 + h = 75 + 10 = 85 \text{ cm}$ of Hg

Let the new length of the trapped air column be $L_2$. Assuming the temperature remains constant, we apply Boyle's law ($P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$). Since the tube has a uniform cross-sectional area, the volume is proportional to the length:

$P_1 L_1 = P_2 L_2$

$65 \times 20 = 85 \times L_2$

$L_2 = \dfrac{1300}{85} = \dfrac{260}{17} \approx 15.29 \text{ cm}$

Rounding to one decimal place, the length of the trapped air column is $15.3 \text{ cm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An open container of volume $50 \text{ cc}$ holds air (mean molecular weight $= 28.8 \text{ g}$) and is exposed to the atmosphere where the pressure is $100 \text{ kPa}$. The container is initially placed in a bath containing boiling water ($100^\circ\text{C}$) until thermal equilibrium is reached. The container is then closed and transferred to a bath containing melting ice ($0^\circ\text{C}$). Determine the pressure of the air inside the container when thermal equilibrium is reached in the melting-ice bath.
questions.solution:
Initially, the open container is in thermal equilibrium with boiling water. Since it is open to the atmosphere, the pressure inside is equal to the atmospheric pressure.

$P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$

$T_1 = 100^\circ\text{C} = 373.15 \text{ K}$

The container is then closed, trapping a fixed mass of air, and placed in a melting ice bath. The volume of the container remains constant.

$T_2 = 0^\circ\text{C} = 273.15 \text{ K}$

Using Gay-Lussac's law for a fixed mass of an ideal gas at constant volume:

$\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$

$P_2 = P_1 \times \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$

$P_2 = 100 \times \dfrac{273.15}{373.15}$

$P_2 \approx 73.2 \text{ kPa}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $100 \text{ cm}$ long glass tube, sealed at both ends, is positioned horizontally with its middle $10 \text{ cm}$ containing mercury. Both ends of the tube enclose air at $27^\circ \text{C}$ under a pressure of $76 \text{ cm}$ of mercury. One air column is subsequently maintained at $0^\circ \text{C}$ and the other at $127^\circ \text{C}$. Determine the length of the air column on the cooler side. Neglect any changes in the volume of the mercury and the glass.
questions.solution:
Total length of the tube is $100 \text{ cm}$ and the length of the mercury column is $10 \text{ cm}$.

Total length of the two air columns is $100 - 10 = 90 \text{ cm}$.

Initially, the mercury is in the middle, so both air columns have equal lengths of $45 \text{ cm}$ at the same temperature and pressure. Thus, the number of moles of air in both columns is equal.

Let the length of the air column on the cooler side ($0^\circ \text{C} = 273 \text{ K}$) be $x$. The length of the air column on the hotter side ($127^\circ \text{C} = 400 \text{ K}$) is $90 - x$.

Since the tube is horizontal, the mercury pellet remains in equilibrium when the pressures on both sides are equal. Let this common pressure be $P$ and the cross-sectional area of the tube be $A$.

Using the ideal gas equation for the cooler side:

$P(xA) = nR(273)$

Using the ideal gas equation for the hotter side:

$P(90 - x)A = nR(400)$

Dividing the two equations:

$\dfrac{x}{90 - x} = \dfrac{273}{400}$

$400x = 273(90 - x)$

$400x = 24570 - 273x$

$673x = 24570$

$x = \dfrac{24570}{673} \approx 36.5 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical tube of length $30\text{ cm}$ is partitioned by a tight-fitting separator, as shown in the figure. The separator can freely slide along the tube and is very weakly conducting. The two parts of the vessel are filled with ideal gases. Initially, the temperatures in parts $A$ and $B$ are $400\text{ K}$ and $100\text{ K}$, respectively, and the separator slides to the momentary equilibrium position indicated. Determine the final equilibrium position of the separator, attained after a long time.
questions.solution:
At the momentary equilibrium, the pressures in both parts are equal. Let this pressure be $P$ and the cross-sectional area of the tube be $S$.

Using the ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$, the number of moles in part A and part B are:

$n_A = \dfrac{P(20S)}{R(400)}$

$n_B = \dfrac{P(10S)}{R(100)}$

The ratio of the number of moles is:

$\dfrac{n_A}{n_B} = \dfrac{20/400}{10/100} = \dfrac{1/20}{1/10} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

After a long time, the system reaches thermal equilibrium because the separator is weakly conducting. The final temperatures in both parts will be equal, say $T$. Since the separator can freely slide, the final pressures will also be equal, say $P'$.

Let the final length of part A be $x$. The final length of part B will be $30 - x$.

Applying the ideal gas equation to the final state for both parts:

$P'(xS) = n_A RT$

$P'((30 - x)S) = n_B RT$

Dividing the two equations gives:

$\dfrac{x}{30 - x} = \dfrac{n_A}{n_B}$

$\dfrac{x}{30 - x} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

$2x = 30 - x$

$3x = 30 \Rightarrow x = 10\text{ cm}$

The final equilibrium position of the separator is $10\text{ cm}$ from the left end.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas is confined between a mercury column and the closed end of a narrow vertical tube of uniform cross-section. The upper end of the tube is open to the atmosphere, and the atmospheric pressure is equal to $76 \text{ cm}$ of mercury. The lengths of the mercury column and the trapped air column are $20 \text{ cm}$ and $43 \text{ cm}$, respectively. Determine the length of the air column after the tube is slowly tilted in a vertical plane through an angle of $60^\circ$. Assume the temperature remains constant.
questions.solution:
Let $A$ be the cross-sectional area of the tube.

In the initial vertical position, the pressure of the trapped air is the sum of the atmospheric pressure and the pressure due to the mercury column.

$P_1 = P_0 + l = 76 + 20 = 96 \text{ cm}$ of Hg

Initial volume of the trapped air, $V_1 = L_1 A = 43A$

When the tube is tilted through an angle of $60^\circ$ with the vertical, the effective vertical height of the mercury column becomes $l \cos 60^\circ$.

Final pressure of the trapped air, $P_2 = P_0 + l \cos 60^\circ$

$P_2 = 76 + 20 \times \dfrac{1}{2} = 86 \text{ cm}$ of Hg

Final volume of the trapped air, $V_2 = L_2 A$

Since the temperature remains constant, applying Boyle's law:

$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$

$96 \times 43A = 86 \times L_2 A$

$L_2 = \dfrac{96 \times 43}{86} = 48 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas is contained in a vessel of volume $V_0$ at temperature $T$ and pressure $p_0$. While maintaining a constant temperature, gas is continuously pumped out of the vessel at a constant volume-rate $dV/dt = r$. The pressure of the extracted gas is equal to the pressure inside the vessel. Determine the pressure of the gas as a function of time.
questions.solution:
Let the mass of the gas in the vessel at time $t$ be $m$.

The density of the gas inside the vessel is $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V_0}$.

The rate at which the volume of gas is pumped out is $r = \dfrac{dV}{dt}$. The corresponding rate at which mass is extracted is given by $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = -\rho r = -\dfrac{m}{V_0} r$.

Rearranging the variables, we get:

$\dfrac{dm}{m} = -\dfrac{r}{V_0} dt$

Integrating both sides from $t = 0$ to $t = t$ and $m = m_0$ to $m = m$:

$\int_{m_0}^{m} \dfrac{dm}{m} = -\dfrac{r}{V_0} \int_{0}^{t} dt$

$\ln\left(\dfrac{m}{m_0}\right) = -\dfrac{rt}{V_0}$

$m = m_0 e^{-rt/V_0}$

From the ideal gas equation, $p V_0 = \dfrac{m}{M} R T$, which implies $p \propto m$ at constant volume and temperature.

Therefore, the pressure as a function of time is:

$p = p_0 e^{-rt/V_0}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the temperature at which the mean speed of the molecules of hydrogen gas becomes equal to the escape speed from the earth.
questions.solution:
The escape speed from the earth is $v_e = 11.2 \times 10^3 \text{ m/s}$.

The mean speed of gas molecules is given by:

$v_{avg} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$

Equating the mean speed of hydrogen molecules to the escape speed:

$\sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}} = v_e$

Squaring both sides and solving for $T$:

$T = \dfrac{\pi M v_e^2}{8R}$

For hydrogen gas, the molar mass is $M = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}$. Substituting the standard values ($R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol K)}$):

$T = \dfrac{3.14 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times (11.2 \times 10^3)^2}{8 \times 8.314}$

$T = \dfrac{3.14 \times 2 \times 125440}{66.512}$

$T \approx 11844 \text{ K}$

The closest value among the given options is $11800 \text{ K}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas is contained in a vessel of volume $V_0$ at temperature $T$ and pressure $p_0$. While maintaining a constant temperature, gas is continuously pumped out of the vessel at a constant volume-rate $dV/dt = r$. The pressure of the extracted gas is equal to the pressure inside the vessel. Determine the time taken before half the original gas is pumped out.
questions.solution:
Let $n$ be the number of moles of gas in the vessel at time $t$.

From the ideal gas equation, $p V_0 = n R T \Rightarrow p = \dfrac{n R T}{V_0}$.

The volume rate of gas pumped out is $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = r$.

The number of moles pumped out in time $dt$ is $dn_{ext} = \dfrac{p dV}{R T} = \dfrac{p r dt}{R T}$.

The rate of decrease of moles in the vessel is $-\dfrac{dn}{dt} = \dfrac{p r}{R T}$.

Substituting $p = \dfrac{n R T}{V_0}$, we get:

$-\dfrac{dn}{dt} = \left(\dfrac{n R T}{V_0}\right) \dfrac{r}{R T} = \dfrac{n r}{V_0}$

Rearranging the variables and integrating:

$\int_{n_0}^{n_0/2} \dfrac{dn}{n} = - \int_0^t \dfrac{r}{V_0} dt$

$\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -\dfrac{r}{V_0} t$

$-\ln 2 = -\dfrac{r}{V_0} t$

$t = \dfrac{V_0 \ln 2}{r}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assume that the surrounding atmospheric pressure stays constant and the air contained in a room (treated as an ideal gas) maintains this pressure by communicating with the surroundings through windows, doors, etc. As the temperature changes between day and night, the internal energy of the air in the room:
options: is directly proportional to the absolute temperature
is directly proportional to the square of the absolute temperature
is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature
questions.solution:
The internal energy of an ideal gas is given by $U = \dfrac{f}{2} n R T$, where $f$ is the degree of freedom, $n$ is the number of moles, $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $T$ is the absolute temperature.

From the ideal gas equation, $P V = n R T$.

Substituting this into the expression for internal energy, we get $U = \dfrac{f}{2} P V$.

Since the room communicates with the surroundings, the pressure $P$ of the air inside the room remains constant and equal to the atmospheric pressure. The volume $V$ of the room is also constant.

Because both $P$ and $V$ are constant, the internal energy $U$ of the air in the room remains constant, irrespective of the temperature changes. As the temperature increases, air escapes the room, causing the number of moles $n$ to decrease such that the product $n T$ remains constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As illustrated in the figure, a cylindrical tube of radius $5 \text{ cm}$ and length $20 \text{ cm}$ is sealed with a tight-fitting cork. The coefficient of friction between the cork and the tube is $0.20$. Inside the tube, an ideal gas is initially at a pressure of $1 \text{ atm}$ and a temperature of $300 \text{ K}$. The tube is subjected to slow heating, and the cork pops out the moment the temperature rises to $600 \text{ K}$. Assuming the gas temperature remains uniform throughout the process, determine $\dfrac{dN}{dl}$, where $dN$ is the magnitude of the normal contact force exerted by a small peripheral length $dl$ of the cork. (Take $1 \text{ atm} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$)
questions.solution:
Let the initial pressure be $P_1 = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$ and initial temperature be $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$.

Since the volume remains constant until the cork pops, $\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$.

$P_2 = 10^5 \times \dfrac{600}{300} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$.

The net outward force exerted by the gas on the cork is balanced by the maximum static friction $f$.

$f = (P_2 - P_{\text{atm}}) \times \pi r^2$

$f = (2 \times 10^5 - 10^5) \times \pi (0.05)^2 = 10^5 \times 0.0025\pi = 250\pi \text{ N}$.

The total friction force is $f = \mu N_{\text{total}}$, where $N_{\text{total}}$ is the total normal force.

$N_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{f}{\mu} = \dfrac{250\pi}{0.20} = 1250\pi \text{ N}$.

The normal force is uniformly distributed over the circumference of the cork.

$\dfrac{dN}{dl} = \dfrac{N_{\text{total}}}{2\pi r}$

$\dfrac{dN}{dl} = \dfrac{1250\pi}{2\pi (0.05)} = \dfrac{1250\pi}{0.1\pi} = 12500 \text{ N/m} = 1.25 \times 10^4 \text{ N/m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical tube of cross-sectional area $A$ is fitted with two frictionless pistons. A metallic wire connects the pistons to each other. Initially, the gas temperature is $T_0$ and its pressure is $p_0$, which is equal to the atmospheric pressure. Determine the tension in the wire if the temperature is increased to $2T_0$.
questions.solution:
Let the initial volume of the gas be $V$. Initially, the pressure of the gas is $p_0$ and the temperature is $T_0$.

When the temperature is increased to $2T_0$, the gas tends to expand. However, the pistons are connected by a metallic wire which becomes taut and restricts any further separation of the pistons. Assuming the thermal expansion of the wire is negligible, the volume of the gas remains constant.

Using the ideal gas law at constant volume ($V_1 = V_2$):
$\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$

Substituting the given values:
$\dfrac{p_0}{T_0} = \dfrac{P_2}{2T_0}$

$\Rightarrow P_2 = 2p_0$

The new pressure of the gas inside the tube is $2p_0$.

Now, considering the equilibrium of one of the pistons, the forces acting on it are:
1. Outward force exerted by the gas = $P_2 A = 2p_0 A$
2. Inward force exerted by the atmospheric pressure = $p_0 A$
3. Inward tension force from the wire = $T$

Equating the forces for equilibrium:
$P_2 A = p_0 A + T$

$2p_0 A = p_0 A + T$

$\Rightarrow T = p_0 A$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas is confined in a long cylindrical vessel equipped with a frictionless piston that has a weight of $1 \text{ kg}$ and a cross-sectional area of $10 \text{ cm}^2$, as shown in the figure. The vessel is placed inside a large chamber holding air at an atmospheric pressure of $100 \text{ kPa}$. The initial length of the gas column is $20 \text{ cm}$. Determine the final length of the gas column if the chamber is subsequently completely evacuated using an exhaust pump. Assume the temperature stays constant throughout the process.
questions.solution:
Let the initial pressure of the gas inside the cylinder be $P_1$.

$P_1 = P_0 + \dfrac{mg}{A}$

Given $P_0 = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $m = 1 \text{ kg}$, and $A = 10 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$.

Taking $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$, the pressure exerted by the piston is:

$\dfrac{mg}{A} = \dfrac{1 \times 10}{10^{-3}} = 10^4 \text{ Pa} = 10 \text{ kPa}$

Therefore, $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa} + 10 \text{ kPa} = 110 \text{ kPa}$.

The initial volume of the gas is $V_1 = A L_1$, where $L_1 = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$.

When the chamber is completely evacuated, the external pressure becomes zero.

The final pressure of the gas is $P_2 = 0 + \dfrac{mg}{A} = 10 \text{ kPa}$.

Let the final length of the gas column be $L_2$. The final volume is $V_2 = A L_2$.

Since the temperature remains constant, Boyle's law applies:

$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$

$P_1 (A L_1) = P_2 (A L_2)$

$P_1 L_1 = P_2 L_2$

$110 \times 0.2 = 10 \times L_2$

$L_2 = \dfrac{110 \times 0.2}{10} = 11 \times 0.2 = 2.2 \text{ m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas is held within a long cylindrical vessel fitted with a frictionless piston of cross-sectional area $10 \text{ cm}^2$ and weight $1 \text{ kg}$. The length of the gas column in the vessel is $20 \text{ cm}$. The atmospheric pressure is $100 \text{ kPa}$. The vessel is subsequently transported into a spaceship orbiting the earth as a satellite. The air pressure inside the spaceship is kept at $100 \text{ kPa}$. Determine the length of the gas column in the cylinder.
questions.solution:
Initial pressure of the gas on Earth is given by $P_1 = P_{atm} + \dfrac{mg}{A}$

Substituting the given values, $P_{atm} = 100 \text{ kPa} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $m = 1 \text{ kg}$, $A = 10 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$, and taking $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$\dfrac{mg}{A} = \dfrac{1 \times 10}{10^{-3}} = 10^4 \text{ Pa} = 10 \text{ kPa}$

$P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa} + 10 \text{ kPa} = 110 \text{ kPa}$

Initial volume of the gas is $V_1 = A L_1$, where $L_1 = 20 \text{ cm}$.

In the orbiting spaceship, the system experiences weightlessness, so the effective weight of the piston is zero.

Final pressure of the gas is $P_2 = P_{atm} = 100 \text{ kPa}$

Assuming the temperature remains constant, Boyle's law is applicable: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$

$P_1 (A L_1) = P_2 (A L_2)$

$110 \times 20 = 100 \times L_2$

$L_2 = \dfrac{110 \times 20}{100} = 22 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two glass bulbs of equal volume, connected by a narrow tube, are initially filled with a gas at $0^\circ C$ and a pressure of $76 \text{ cm}$ of mercury. One bulb is subsequently immersed in melting ice, while the other is placed in a water bath maintained at $62^\circ C$. Assuming the volume of the connecting tube is negligible, determine the new value of the pressure inside the bulbs.
questions.solution:
Let the volume of each bulb be $V$.

Initial total number of moles of gas is given by the ideal gas equation:

$n = \dfrac{P_1 (2V)}{R T_1}$

where $P_1 = 76 \text{ cm}$ of Hg and $T_1 = 273 \text{ K}$.

When the bulbs are maintained at temperatures $T_2 = 273 \text{ K}$ (melting ice) and $T_3 = 273 + 62 = 335 \text{ K}$ (water bath), let the new pressure be $P'$. The total number of moles remains constant.

$n_1 + n_2 = n$

$\dfrac{P' V}{R T_2} + \dfrac{P' V}{R T_3} = \dfrac{P_1 (2V)}{R T_1}$

Substituting the given values:

$\dfrac{P' V}{R \times 273} + \dfrac{P' V}{R \times 335} = \dfrac{76 \times 2V}{R \times 273}$

$P' \left( \dfrac{1}{273} + \dfrac{1}{335} \right) = \dfrac{152}{273}$

$P' \left( \dfrac{335 + 273}{273 \times 335} \right) = \dfrac{152}{273}$

$P' \left( \dfrac{608}{335} \right) = 152$

$P' = \dfrac{152 \times 335}{608}$

Since $152 \times 4 = 608$:

$P' = \dfrac{335}{4} = 83.75 \text{ cm}$ of Hg

Rounding to the nearest integer, the new pressure is approximately $84 \text{ cm}$ of mercury.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an experiment, an ideal gas is observed to obey an additional law $pV^2 = \text{constant}$. The gas is initially at a volume $V$ and temperature $T$. Determine its temperature when it expands to a volume $2V$.
questions.solution:
For an ideal gas, the equation of state is $pV = nRT$, which gives $p = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$.

Given the process follows the law $pV^2 = \text{constant}$.

Substituting the expression for $p$ into the given law:

$\left(\dfrac{nRT}{V}\right)V^2 = \text{constant}$

$TV = \text{constant}$

This implies $T_1 V_1 = T_2 V_2$.

Given $T_1 = T$, $V_1 = V$, and $V_2 = 2V$:

$T \times V = T_2 \times (2V)$

$T_2 = \dfrac{T}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A large closed cylindrical tank contains water. Initially, the trapped air above the water surface is at a pressure of $2p_0$ ($p_0$ being the atmospheric pressure) and occupies a height $h_0$. A hole is located in the tank wall at a depth $h_1$ below the top, from which water escapes. A long vertical tube, open to the atmosphere, is connected to the lower part of the tank. Determine the height of the water level in the long tube above the top of the tank when water ceases to flow out of the hole.
questions.solution:
When water ceases to flow out of the hole, the fluid system reaches a state of static equilibrium.

For the flow to stop, the pressure of the water just inside the hole must be equal to the external atmospheric pressure, $p_0$.

The long vertical tube is open to the atmosphere, which means the pressure at the free surface of the water in this tube is also $p_0$.

In a continuous body of static fluid, points that are at the same pressure must be at the same horizontal level. Since both the free surface in the long tube and the hole are at pressure $p_0$, they must lie on the same horizontal plane.

The hole is located at a depth $h_1$ below the top of the tank. Therefore, its position relative to the top of the tank is $-h_1$.

Consequently, the height of the water level in the long tube above the top of the tank is also $-h_1$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A weather report states, "Temperature $20^\circ C$ : Relative humidity $100\%". Determine the dew point.
questions.solution:
Relative humidity is defined as the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor in the air to the saturated vapor pressure of water at the same temperature.

When the relative humidity is $100\%$, the air is completely saturated with water vapor. This means the partial pressure of water vapor is equal to the saturated vapor pressure at the current temperature.

The dew point is the temperature at which the air becomes saturated with water vapor. Since the air is already saturated at the given temperature of $20^\circ C$, the dew point is equal to the current temperature.

Therefore, the dew point is $20^\circ C$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A large closed cylindrical tank contains water. At a certain instant, the air trapped above the water surface is at a pressure of $2p_0$ (where $p_0$ is the atmospheric pressure) and has a height $h_0$. Water flows out from a hole in the tank wall located at a depth $h_1$ below the top. A long vertical tube, open to the atmosphere, is connected to the lower part of the tank. Assuming the density of water is $\rho$, calculate the initial speed at which water emerges from the hole.
questions.solution:
Let us apply Bernoulli's principle between the free surface of the water inside the tank and the point just outside the hole.

At the free surface of the water inside the tank:
Pressure, $P_1 = 2p_0$
Velocity, $v_1 \approx 0$ (since the tank is large)
Height of the water surface from the hole, $h = h_1 - h_0$

At the hole (just outside):
Pressure, $P_2 = p_0$ (atmospheric pressure)
Velocity of efflux, $v_2 = v$

According to Bernoulli's equation:
$P_1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g h = P_2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + 0$

Substituting the values, we get:
$2p_0 + 0 + \rho g (h_1 - h_0) = p_0 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$

Rearranging the terms to solve for $v$:
$\dfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = 2p_0 - p_0 + \rho g (h_1 - h_0)$

$\dfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = p_0 + \rho g (h_1 - h_0)$

$v^2 = \dfrac{2}{\rho} [p_0 + \rho g (h_1 - h_0)]$

$v = \left[ \dfrac{2}{\rho} (p_0 + \rho g (h_1 - h_0)) \right]^{1/2}$

The vertical tube open to the atmosphere acts as a manometer and does not affect the initial speed of efflux.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The air condition inside a closed room is described as follows: temperature $= 25^\circ \text{C}$, relative humidity $= 60\%$, and pressure $= 104 \text{ kPa}$. If all the water vapour is extracted from the room without altering the temperature, determine the new pressure. The saturation vapour pressure at $25^\circ \text{C}$ is $3.2 \text{ kPa}$.
questions.solution:
Relative humidity is given by the ratio of partial pressure of water vapour to the saturation vapour pressure.

$RH = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{sat}}$

$0.60 = \dfrac{P_v}{3.2}$

$P_v = 1.92 \text{ kPa}$

The total pressure of the moist air is the sum of the partial pressure of dry air and the partial pressure of water vapour.

$P_{total} = P_a + P_v$

$104 = P_a + 1.92$

$P_a = 102.08 \text{ kPa}$

When all the water vapour is extracted at constant temperature and volume, the new pressure in the room is simply the partial pressure of the dry air.

New pressure $= 102.08 \text{ kPa}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an open room, the temperature is $20^\circ C$ and the dew point is $10^\circ C$. If the temperature of the room falls to $15^\circ C$, determine the new dew point.
questions.solution:
The dew point is the temperature at which the water vapor present in the air becomes saturated. It depends only on the partial pressure of water vapor in the air.

In an open room, the total pressure and the mole fraction of water vapor remain constant. As the temperature falls from $20^\circ C$ to $15^\circ C$, no condensation occurs because the new temperature is still above the initial dew point of $10^\circ C$.

Since no moisture is added or removed and the partial pressure of water vapor remains constant, the temperature required to saturate the air remains the same.

Therefore, the dew point remains unchanged at $10^\circ C$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Pure water vapour is enclosed in a vessel of volume $10 \text{ cm}^3$ at a relative humidity of $40\%$. The vapour is slowly compressed isothermally. Determine the volume of the vapour at which condensation starts.
questions.solution:
Relative humidity is given by $RH = \dfrac{P}{P_s} \times 100\%$, where $P$ is the partial pressure of water vapour and $P_s$ is the saturated vapour pressure.

Given $RH = 40\%$, the initial pressure of the vapour is $P_1 = 0.4 P_s$.

Condensation begins when the pressure of the vapour reaches the saturated vapour pressure, so the final pressure is $P_2 = P_s$.

Since the process is isothermal, temperature is constant. Using Boyle's law for the water vapour:

$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$

Substituting the given values:

$0.4 P_s \times 10 = P_s \times V_2$

$V_2 = 4 \text{ cm}^3$

Thus, condensation starts when the volume is $4 \text{ cm}^3$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Using the figure, determine the boiling point of methyl alcohol at $1 \text{ atm}$ ($760 \text{ mm}$ of mercury) and at $0.5 \text{ atm}$.
questions.solution:
The boiling point of a liquid is the temperature at which its vapour pressure equals the external atmospheric pressure.

For methyl alcohol, the normal boiling point at $1 \text{ atm}$ ($760 \text{ mm}$ of Hg) is known to be $65^\circ \text{C}$.

When the external pressure is reduced to $0.5 \text{ atm}$ ($380 \text{ mm}$ of Hg), the boiling point decreases. Based on the standard vapour pressure curve of methyl alcohol, the temperature at which its vapour pressure is $0.5 \text{ atm}$ is $48^\circ \text{C}$.

Thus, the boiling points are $65^\circ \text{C}$ and $48^\circ \text{C}$ respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A barometer tube has a length of $80 \text{ cm}$ (above the mercury reservoir). On a particular day, it reads $76 \text{ cm}$. A small quantity of water is introduced into the tube, causing the reading to drop to $75.4 \text{ cm}$. Determine the relative humidity in the space above the mercury column, given that the saturation vapour pressure at room temperature is $1.0 \text{ cm}$.
questions.solution:
Initial reading of the barometer, $h_1 = 76 \text{ cm}$.

This implies the atmospheric pressure is $P_{\text{atm}} = 76 \text{ cm of Hg}$, and the space above the mercury column is initially a vacuum (pressure = $0$).

When a small quantity of water is introduced, it evaporates and exerts a vapour pressure $P_v$ in the space above the mercury.

The new reading of the barometer is given as $h_2 = 75.4 \text{ cm}$.

Equating the pressures at the base of the tube:

$P_{\text{atm}} = h_2 + P_v$

$76 = 75.4 + P_v$

$P_v = 76 - 75.4 = 0.6 \text{ cm of Hg}$

The actual vapour pressure of water in the space is $0.6 \text{ cm of Hg}$.

The saturation vapour pressure (SVP) at room temperature is given as $1.0 \text{ cm of Hg}$.

Relative humidity (RH) is defined as the ratio of the actual vapour pressure to the saturation vapour pressure:

$\text{RH} = \dfrac{P_v}{\text{SVP}} \times 100\%$

$\text{RH} = \dfrac{0.6}{1.0} \times 100\% = 60\%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The average temperature of the human body is $98 \text{ }^\circ F$. Assuming that the vapour pressure of the blood in the veins behaves like that of pure water, determine the minimum atmospheric pressure required to prevent the blood from boiling. Use the appropriate vapour pressure graph for water.
questions.solution:
The temperature of the human body is given as $98^{\circ}\text{F}$.

Converting this temperature to the Celsius scale:

$T(^{\circ}\text{C}) = \dfrac{5}{9}(T(^{\circ}\text{F}) - 32)$

$T(^{\circ}\text{C}) = \dfrac{5}{9}(98 - 32) = \dfrac{5}{9}(66) = 36.67^{\circ}\text{C} \approx 37^{\circ}\text{C}$

For a liquid to prevent boiling, the external atmospheric pressure must be greater than or equal to the vapour pressure of the liquid at that temperature. The vapour pressure of pure water at $37^{\circ}\text{C}$ is approximately $47 \text{ mm Hg}$.

Among the given options, the closest value for the minimum atmospheric pressure required to prevent the blood from boiling is $50 \text{ mm Hg}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A glass holds some water at a room temperature of $20^\circ\text{C}$. Refrigerated water is slowly added to it. Once the glass temperature drops to $10^\circ\text{C}$, tiny droplets begin to condense on its outer surface. Determine the relative humidity in the room. The boiling point of water is $20^\circ\text{C}$ at a pressure of $17.5\text{ mm}$ of mercury, and $10^\circ\text{C}$ at $8.9\text{ mm}$ of mercury.
questions.solution:
The dew point of the air in the room is the temperature at which condensation begins, which is $10^{\circ}\text{C}$.

The partial pressure of water vapor in the room is equal to the saturated vapor pressure at the dew point.

Since the boiling point of water is $10^{\circ}\text{C}$ at $8.9\text{ mm}$ of Hg, the saturated vapor pressure at $10^{\circ}\text{C}$ is $8.9\text{ mm}$ of Hg.

Similarly, the saturated vapor pressure at the room temperature of $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ is $17.5\text{ mm}$ of Hg.

Relative humidity is given by the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor to the saturated vapor pressure at room temperature.

$\text{Relative Humidity} = \dfrac{8.9}{17.5} \times 100\%$

$\text{Relative Humidity} \approx 50.85\% \approx 51\%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $50 \text{ m}^3$ volume of saturated vapour is cooled from $30^\circ C$ to $20^\circ C$. Calculate the mass of the condensed water. At $30^\circ C$, the absolute humidity of saturated water vapour is $30 \text{ g m}^{-3}$, while at $20^\circ C$ it is $16 \text{ g m}^{-3}$.
questions.solution:
The initial mass of water vapour present in the volume at $30^{\circ} \text{C}$ is given by the product of volume and absolute humidity.

$m_{1} = 50 \text{ m}^{3} \times 30 \text{ g m}^{-3} = 1500 \text{ g}$

When the vapour is cooled to $20^{\circ} \text{C}$, the maximum mass of water vapour that can be held in the same volume is:

$m_{2} = 50 \text{ m}^{3} \times 16 \text{ g m}^{-3} = 800 \text{ g}$

The excess water vapour will condense into liquid water. The mass of the condensed water is:

$\Delta m = m_{1} - m_{2} = 1500 \text{ g} - 800 \text{ g} = 700 \text{ g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A barometer accurately indicates the atmospheric pressure as $76 \text{ cm}$ of mercury. Using a dropper, water droplets are slowly introduced into the barometer tube. The height of the mercury column initially decreases before becoming constant. Given that the saturation vapour pressure at the prevailing atmospheric temperature is $0.80 \text{ cm}$ of mercury, determine the height of the mercury column when it attains its minimum value.
questions.solution:
Initially, the space above the mercury column in the barometer is a vacuum, so the pressure is zero. The atmospheric pressure is balanced by the mercury column alone:

$P_{atm} = h_0$

When water droplets are introduced into the tube, they evaporate and exert a vapour pressure $P_v$ in the space above the mercury. The pressure at the level of the free surface of the mercury in the reservoir must still equal the atmospheric pressure. Therefore, the new equilibrium condition is:

$P_{atm} = P_v + h$

As more water is added, $P_v$ increases and the height of the mercury column $h$ decreases. The height of the mercury column reaches its minimum constant value when the vapour pressure reaches its maximum possible value, which is the saturation vapour pressure of water at that temperature.

Given $P_{atm} = 76 \text{ cm}$ of Hg and the saturation vapour pressure $P_v = 0.80 \text{ cm}$ of Hg, we have:

$76 = 0.80 + h$

$h = 76 - 0.80 = 75.2 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One mole of an ideal gas undergoes a process described by $p = \dfrac{p_0}{1 + (V/V_0)^2}$, where $p_0$ and $V_0$ represent constants. Determine the temperature of the gas when $V = V_0$.
questions.solution:
The equation of the process is given by $p = \dfrac{p_0}{1 + (V/V_0)^2}$.

Substituting $V = V_0$ into the given equation:

$p = \dfrac{p_0}{1 + (V_0/V_0)^2} = \dfrac{p_0}{1 + 1} = \dfrac{p_0}{2}$

For one mole of an ideal gas, the ideal gas equation is $pV = RT$.

Substituting the values of $p$ and $V$ at this state:

$\left(\dfrac{p_0}{2}\right) V_0 = RT$

$T = \dfrac{p_0 V_0}{2R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inverted gas jar over water collects $50 \text{ cc}$ of oxygen. The room temperature is $27^\circ \text{C}$ and the atmospheric pressure is $99.4 \text{ kPa}$. The water level inside the jar is equal to the level outside. Given that the saturation vapour pressure at $27^\circ \text{C}$ is $3.4 \text{ kPa}$, determine the number of moles of oxygen collected in the jar.
questions.solution:
Since the water level inside the gas jar is equal to the level outside, the total pressure inside the jar is equal to the atmospheric pressure.

$P_{ ext{total}} = 99.4 \text{ kPa}$

The gas collected over water is a mixture of oxygen and water vapour. The partial pressure of oxygen is obtained by subtracting the aqueous tension (saturation vapour pressure of water) from the total pressure:

$P_{\text{O}_2} = P_{\text{total}} - P_{\text{water}}$

$P_{\text{O}_2} = 99.4 \text{ kPa} - 3.4 \text{ kPa} = 96.0 \text{ kPa} = 96 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$

Given:
Volume, $V = 50 \text{ cc} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
Temperature, $T = 27^\circ \text{C} = 300 \text{ K}$
Universal gas constant, $R \approx 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$

Using the ideal gas equation, $P_{\text{O}_2} V = n R T$:

$n = \dfrac{P_{\text{O}_2} V}{R T}$

$n = \dfrac{96 \times 10^3 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{8.314 \times 300}$

$n = \dfrac{4.8}{2494.2} \approx 1.925 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}$

Rounding to the closest given option, we get $1.93 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A faulty barometer contains a certain amount of air and saturated water vapour. It reads $74.0 \text{ cm}$ when the atmospheric pressure is $76.0 \text{ cm}$ of mercury, and it reads $72.10 \text{ cm}$ when the atmospheric pressure is $74.0 \text{ cm}$ of mercury. The saturation vapour pressure at the air temperature is $1.0 \text{ cm}$ of mercury. Determine the length of the barometer tube above the mercury level in the reservoir.
questions.solution:
Let $L$ be the length of the barometer tube above the mercury level in the reservoir.

Let $P_{a}$ be the pressure of the air inside the tube and $P_{v}$ be the saturation vapour pressure.

The atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$ balances the mercury column $h$ and the pressure inside the tube:

$P_{atm} = h + P_{a} + P_{v}$

$\Rightarrow P_{a} = P_{atm} - h - P_{v}$

For the first case:

$P_{atm1} = 76.0 \text{ cm}$, $h_{1} = 74.0 \text{ cm}$, $P_{v} = 1.0 \text{ cm}$

$P_{a1} = 76.0 - 74.0 - 1.0 = 1.0 \text{ cm}$

The length of the air column is $L_{1} = L - 74.0$.

For the second case:

$P_{atm2} = 74.0 \text{ cm}$, $h_{2} = 72.1 \text{ cm}$, $P_{v} = 1.0 \text{ cm}$

$P_{a2} = 74.0 - 72.1 - 1.0 = 0.9 \text{ cm}$

The length of the air column is $L_{2} = L - 72.1$.

Since the temperature is constant, Boyle's law applies to the trapped air:

$P_{a1}L_{1} = P_{a2}L_{2}$

$1.0(L - 74.0) = 0.9(L - 72.1)$

$L - 74.0 = 0.9L - 64.89$

$0.1L = 74.0 - 64.89 = 9.11$

$L = 91.1 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During a winter day, the external temperature is $0^\circ\text{C}$ with a relative humidity of $40\%$. This outside air enters a room where it is heated to $20^\circ\text{C}$. Determine the relative humidity inside the room. The saturation vapour pressure is $4.6 \text{ mm}$ of mercury at $0^\circ\text{C}$ and $18 \text{ mm}$ of mercury at $20^\circ\text{C}$.
questions.solution:
The relative humidity (RH) is defined as the ratio of the actual vapour pressure to the saturation vapour pressure at a given temperature.

Given:
External temperature, $T_1 = 0^\circ\text{C}$
Relative humidity at $0^\circ\text{C}$, $\text{RH}_1 = 40\%$
Saturation vapour pressure at $0^\circ\text{C}$, $P_{s1} = 4.6 \text{ mm}$ of Hg

The actual vapour pressure of the outside air at $0^\circ\text{C}$ is:
$P_{v1} = \dfrac{\text{RH}_1}{100} \times P_{s1} = \dfrac{40}{100} \times 4.6 = 1.84 \text{ mm}$ of Hg

When the outside air enters the room and is heated to $20^\circ\text{C}$, the process occurs at a constant atmospheric pressure. Since the mole fraction of the water vapour in the air mixture remains constant during this sensible heating, its partial pressure also remains constant.

Therefore, the actual vapour pressure inside the room at $20^\circ\text{C}$ is:
$P_{v2} = P_{v1} = 1.84 \text{ mm}$ of Hg

Given the saturation vapour pressure at $20^\circ\text{C}$ is $P_{s2} = 18 \text{ mm}$ of Hg, the new relative humidity inside the room is:
$\text{RH}_2 = \left( \dfrac{P_{v2}}{P_{s2}} \right) \times 100\%$

$\text{RH}_2 = \left( \dfrac{1.84}{18} \right) \times 100\% \approx 10.22\%$

Rounding to one decimal place, the relative humidity inside the room is $10.2\%$.

Answer: $10.2\%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a cylindrical tube of cross-sectional area $A$ is fitted with two frictionless pistons. A metallic wire connects the pistons to each other. Initially, the gas temperature is $T_0$ and its pressure is $p_0$, which is equal to the atmospheric pressure. Determine the tension in the wire.
questions.solution:
Let $T$ be the tension in the metallic wire connecting the two pistons.

Consider the equilibrium of one of the pistons, for instance, the left piston. The forces acting on it along the horizontal direction are:
1. The force due to the gas pressure from inside the tube, acting outwards (to the left), which is equal to $p_0 A$.
2. The force due to the atmospheric pressure from outside, acting inwards (to the right), which is also equal to $p_0 A$.
3. The tension $T$ in the wire, pulling the piston inwards (to the right).

Since the piston is frictionless and in equilibrium, the net force acting on it must be zero. Equating the forces acting in opposite directions, we get:

$p_0 A = p_0 A + T$

Solving for $T$, we obtain:

$T = 0$

Therefore, the tension in the wire is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A room has a volume of $50 \text{ m}^3$, where the temperature is $300 \text{ K}$ and the relative humidity is $20\%$. Given that the saturation vapour pressure at $300 \text{ K}$ is $3.3 \text{ kPa}$, determine the mass of the water vapour contained within the room.
questions.solution:
Relative humidity is the ratio of partial pressure of water vapour to the saturation vapour pressure at the same temperature.

$RH = \dfrac{P}{P_{s}}$

$0.20 = \dfrac{P}{3.3 \times 10^{3}}$

$P = 0.20 \times 3.3 \times 10^{3} = 660 \text{ Pa}$

Using the ideal gas equation, $PV = nRT = \dfrac{m}{M}RT$

$m = \dfrac{PVM}{RT}$

Substituting $V = 50 \text{ m}^{3}$, $M = 18 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$ and $T = 300 \text{ K}$:

$m = \dfrac{660 \times 50 \times 18}{8.314 \times 300}$

$m = \dfrac{594000}{2494.2} \approx 238 \text{ g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: On a given day, the temperature and humidity of air are $27^\circ \text{C}$ and $50\%$. Determine the amount of vapour that must be added to $1$ cubic metre of air to saturate it. The saturation vapour pressure at $27^\circ \text{C}$ is $3600 \text{ Pa}$.
questions.solution:
Relative humidity is given as $50\%$.

Initial vapour pressure $P_{1} = 0.50 \times 3600 = 1800 \text{ Pa}$.

To saturate the air, the vapour pressure must reach the saturation vapour pressure $P_{s} = 3600 \text{ Pa}$.

Additional vapour pressure required is $\Delta P = 3600 - 1800 = 1800 \text{ Pa}$.

Using the ideal gas equation, $\Delta P V = \dfrac{\Delta m}{M} R T$, where $V = 1 \text{ m}^{3}$, $T = 300 \text{ K}$, and $M = 18 \text{ g/mol}$ is the molar mass of water.

$\Delta m = \dfrac{\Delta P V M}{R T}$

$\Delta m = \dfrac{1800 \times 1 \times 18}{8.314 \times 300} \text{ g}$

$\Delta m = \dfrac{108}{8.314} \approx 13 \text{ g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a large closed cylindrical tank contains water. Initially, the air trapped above the water surface has a pressure of $2p_0$ (where $p_0$ is the atmospheric pressure) and occupies a height $h_0$. A hole is present in the wall of the tank at a depth $h_1$ below the top, allowing water to flow out. A long vertical tube, open to the atmosphere, is connected to the tank. Determine the initial height $h_2$ of the water level in the long tube above the top of the tank. Let $ ho$ be the density of water.
questions.solution:
Let us consider the pressure at the horizontal level of the water surface inside the tank.

The pressure of the trapped air above the water surface in the tank is given as $2p_0$. Therefore, the pressure at the water surface inside the tank is $2p_0$.

The water surface in the tank is at a depth $h_0$ below the top of the tank.
The water level in the open vertical tube is at a height $h_2$ above the top of the tank.
Thus, the total vertical depth of the water surface in the tank below the free surface in the open tube is $h_2 + h_0$.

Since the tank is large, the velocity of the water inside it is negligible, and we can apply the principles of hydrostatics. The pressure at the free surface of the open tube is atmospheric pressure, $p_0$.

Using the hydrostatic pressure equation, the pressure at the level of the water surface in the tank, calculated from the tube side, is:
$P = p_0 + \rho g (h_2 + h_0)$

Equating this to the pressure of the trapped air inside the tank:
$p_0 + \rho g (h_2 + h_0) = 2p_0$

$\rho g (h_2 + h_0) = p_0$

$h_2 + h_0 = \dfrac{p_0}{\rho g}$

$h_2 = \dfrac{p_0}{\rho g} - h_0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a room of volume $50 \text{ m}^3$, the temperature is $300 \text{ K}$ and the relative humidity is $20\%$. After the floor is washed with water, $500 \text{ g}$ of water remains sticking to the floor. Assuming there is no communication with the surroundings, determine the relative humidity when the floor dries. Changes in temperature and pressure can be neglected. The saturation vapour pressure at $300 \text{ K}$ is $3.3 \text{ kPa}$.
questions.solution:
Initial vapour pressure $P_1 = 0.20 \times 3.3 \text{ kPa} = 0.66 \text{ kPa} = 660 \text{ Pa}$.

Number of moles of water evaporated $n = \dfrac{500}{18} \text{ mol}$.

Increase in vapour pressure due to evaporated water is given by the ideal gas equation:

$\Delta P = \dfrac{nRT}{V} = \dfrac{500 \times 8.314 \times 300}{18 \times 50} \text{ Pa} \approx 1386 \text{ Pa} = 1.386 \text{ kPa}$.

Final vapour pressure $P_2 = P_1 + \Delta P = 0.66 \text{ kPa} + 1.386 \text{ kPa} = 2.046 \text{ kPa}$.

Final relative humidity is:

$RH = \dfrac{P_2}{P_s} \times 100 = \dfrac{2.046}{3.3} \times 100 \approx 62\%$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bucket full of water is placed in a room at $15^\circ\text{C}$ with an initial relative humidity of $40\%$. The volume of the room is $50 \text{ m}^3$. The saturation vapour pressures of water at $15^\circ\text{C}$ and $20^\circ\text{C}$ are $1.6 \text{ kPa}$ and $2.4 \text{ kPa}$ respectively. Assuming the room has already reached saturation at $15^\circ\text{C}$, if the room temperature is subsequently increased by $5^\circ\text{C}$, how much additional water will evaporate?
questions.solution:
Let the mass of water vapour in the room at saturation at $15^\circ\text{C}$ be $m_1$. Using the ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$, we have:

$n_1 = \dfrac{P_1 V}{R T_1}$

$\Rightarrow m_1 = \dfrac{P_1 V M}{R T_1}$

Substituting the given values ($P_1 = 1.6 \text{ kPa} = 1600 \text{ Pa}$, $V = 50 \text{ m}^3$, $T_1 = 288 \text{ K}$, $M = 18 \text{ g/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol K)}$):

$m_1 = \dfrac{1600 \times 50 \times 18}{8.314 \times 288} \approx 601.4 \text{ g}$

When the temperature is increased to $20^\circ\text{C}$ ($293 \text{ K}$), the new saturation vapour pressure is $P_2 = 2.4 \text{ kPa} = 2400 \text{ Pa}$. The mass of water vapour at saturation at $20^\circ\text{C}$ is:

$m_2 = \dfrac{P_2 V M}{R T_2}$

$m_2 = \dfrac{2400 \times 50 \times 18}{8.314 \times 293} \approx 886.7 \text{ g}$

The additional amount of water that will evaporate is the difference between the mass of water vapour at saturation at $20^\circ\text{C}$ and $15^\circ\text{C}$:

$\Delta m = m_2 - m_1 = 886.7 - 601.4 = 285.3 \text{ g}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $285 \text{ g}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bucket full of water is placed in a room at $15^\circ\text{C}$ with an initial relative humidity of $40\%$. The volume of the room is $50 \text{ m}^3$. The saturation vapour pressures of water at $15^\circ\text{C}$ and $20^\circ\text{C}$ are $1.6 \text{ kPa}$ and $2.4 \text{ kPa}$ respectively. Determine the mass of water that will evaporate to bring the room to saturation at $15^\circ\text{C}$.
questions.solution:
The initial vapour pressure of water in the room is given by:

$P_{i} = \text{Relative Humidity} \times \text{Saturation Vapour Pressure}$

$P_{i} = 0.40 \times 1.6 \text{ kPa} = 0.64 \text{ kPa}$

To bring the room to saturation, the final vapour pressure must be equal to the saturation vapour pressure at $15^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$P_{f} = 1.6 \text{ kPa}$

The required change in vapour pressure is:

$\Delta P = P_{f} - P_{i} = 1.6 - 0.64 = 0.96 \text{ kPa} = 960 \text{ Pa}$

Using the ideal gas equation, $PV = nRT = \dfrac{m}{M}RT$, the mass of water evaporated is given by:

$\Delta m = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V \cdot M}{RT}$

Substituting the given values ($V = 50 \text{ m}^3$, $M = 18 \text{ g/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol K)}$, $T = 288 \text{ K}$):

$\Delta m = \dfrac{960 \times 50 \times 18}{8.314 \times 288}$

$\Delta m = \dfrac{864000}{2394.432} \approx 360.8 \text{ g}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, the mass of water that will evaporate is $361 \text{ g}$.
